channel.title: Latest alerts from washingtonpost.com
channel.description: An unofficial feed created by Derek Willis.

item.title: Analysis: Ranking the 2028 Democratic presidential contenders
item.description: The possibilities include governors, senators, House members and even celebrities. And although no one has officially declared their candidacy yet, some are sending strong signals that they'll run.

item.title: Correction: Scouts say they will continue to allow trans children to join. An earlier alert said they would be denied entry under new rules.
item.description: In announcing the Pentagon’s agreement with Scouting America, the defense secretary assailed the group for welcoming transgender children but stopped short of saying they would be denied entry. A previous alert incorrectly said that the organization would ban transgender children. This alert has been corrected.

item.title: Neil Sedaka dies at 86. The singer and songwriter was a pop hitmaker in two eras.
item.description: He rose to stardom in the 1950s with hits like “Breaking Up Is Hard to Do,” then made a roaring comeback in the ’70s with “Laughter in the Rain” and “Love Will Keep Us Together.”

item.title: Trump orders U.S. government to drop use of Anthropic’s technology, in dispute over access to its AI
item.description: President Donald Trump made a dramatic intervention in the dispute between Anthropic and the Pentagon over the use of AI for autonomous weapons or surveillance.

item.title: Pete Hegseth strikes new deal with Scouts: Girls allowed for now, DEI and transgender children banned
item.description: The defense secretary said Scouting America made the concessions to preserve its long-standing relationship with the military but will remain under review.

item.title: Bill Clinton tells GOP-led panel he had no knowledge of Jeffrey Epstein’s crimes
item.description: The appearance in New York marks the first time a former president has been compelled to testify before Congress under a subpoena.

item.title: Carolyn Hax chat: My mom tries to ruin my birthday every year
item.description: Carolyn Hax chat: My mom tries to ruin my birthday every year

item.title: A new car, home feel out of reach for middle-class Americans, poll finds
item.description: Most renters — including those earning six figures — doubt they could afford a home in the foreseeable future.

item.title: The hypothetical nuclear attack that escalated the Pentagon’s showdown with Anthropic
item.description: Start-up Anthropic and the U.S. military are careening toward a clash over government use of artificial intelligence — and whether it should be allowed to kill.

item.title: 10 questions, and don't forget the bonus wager! Finish off your week with On the Record.
item.description: Think you’ve got what it takes to earn a perfect score? Take today’s longer news quiz and complete your point total for the week.

item.title: Election powers. Refugee death. Iran talks. Hillary Clinton. Anthropic showdown. Snow maps. Neanderthal mating. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: 'No chance' that military strikes against Iran would lead to drawn-out war, Vance says
item.description: In an interview with The Washington Post, Vance said he remains a “skeptic of foreign military interventions.”

item.title: Anthropic rejects Pentagon’s terms for lethal use of its chatbot Claude, setting up clash with Hegseth
item.description: CEO Dario Amodei said Anthropic could not permit its technology to be applied to domestic mass surveillance or fully autonomous weapons. The Pentagon gave Anthropic a 5:01 p.m. Friday deadline to comply or risk being forced to provide full access to its AI under the Defense Production Act.

item.title: Netflix won't raise bid for Warner Bros., clearing a path for Paramount to take over the studio
item.description: The streaming giant said it will not increase its bid to acquire Warner Bros. Discovery, potentially paving the way for Paramount Skydance to swoop in and buy the company after a tense war between two media mega-corporations.

item.title: DOJ reviewing whether Epstein files with Trump allegations were wrongly withheld
item.description: Three summaries of interviews the FBI conducted in 2019 with a woman who had accused Donald Trump of sexually assaulting her are missing from the files related to convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, multiple news outlets have reported. The woman had accused Trump of sexually assaulting her decades earlier while she was a minor. No evidence has emerged publicly to corroborate that accusation.

item.title: Neanderthal males and human females had babies together, ancient DNA reveals
item.description: Scientists at the University of Pennsylvania examined Neanderthal DNA and modern human DNA to find evidence that couplings between these species followed the same parental pattern over interbreeding events separated by 200,000 years.

item.title: Trump’s White House ballroom project can continue for now, judge rules
item.description: The National Trust, which has sued to stop the ballroom, said it would immediately revise its complaint and try again.

item.title: Judge rules IRS broke federal law ‘approximately 42,695 times’ in giving data to DHS
item.description: The judge wrote that the vast majority of the nearly 47,300 taxpayer addresses the IRS shared with Immigration and Customs Enforcement in August were disclosed without the IRS confirming that ICE provided a valid address for the person whose records it was seeking.

item.title: Trump, seeking executive power over elections, is urged to declare emergency
item.description: Activists who say they are in coordination with the White House are circulating a draft executive order that would unlock extraordinary presidential power over voting.

item.title: Analysis: Trump stirred rumors for years. Now it’s the Clintons’ turn to speak about Epstein, in testimony before a House panel.
item.description: Bill and Hillary Clinton have survived decades of scrutiny and conspiracy theories. Often, their adversaries come out on the losing end.

item.title: RFK Jr.’s war on vaccines is coming for the elderly
item.description: The miseries of aging are more linked to late-life infections than was previously understood.

item.title: Catering to ‘the worst qualities of mankind’ in today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Thursday points.

item.title: Cuba boat shooting. Republican trap. Hillary Clinton. MAHA. Erika Kirk. Plane seating. Dogs in Congress. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: Trump administration to withhold $259 million in Medicaid funds from Minnesota, citing fraud
item.description: The move, announced by Vice President JD Vance, targets alleged health care fraud and signals a broader crackdown that could extend to other states.

item.title: A professor challenged the Smithsonian. Security shut the gallery.
item.description: As President Donald Trump seeks to reshape its museums and other cultural institutions, wall text has become a battleground and documentation a form of resistance.

item.title: Trump policy of deporting migrants to countries where they are not citizens is unlawful, judge rules
item.description: The ruling could make it more difficult for the federal government, which immigration experts said has sent thousands of migrants to countries that are not their own, to continue to use the practice as widely.

item.title: Cuban forces shoot 4 dead on U.S. speedboat, Havana says
item.description: The crew of an American speedboat opened fire on Cuban troops, the country’s Interior Ministry said. The Cuban forces returned fire, killing four and wounding six.

item.title: Trump Treasury official to exit after privately objecting to White House-backed push to target alleged fraud in Minnesota
item.description: The departure of John Hurley, a donor to President Donald Trump who is serving as the undersecretary for terrorism and financial intelligence, underscores how some Trump allies in the federal government are uneasy with the extent of White House directives to deploy law enforcement tools against Minneapolis’s Somali community.

item.title: Lawrence Summers resigns from teaching at Harvard over ties to Jeffrey Epstein
item.description: The former U.S. treasury secretary and former Harvard University president’s decision follows a review of documents that showed the nature of his relationship with the disgraced financier.

item.title: Don’t save Social Security
item.description: Trying to fix the retirement program is infeasible. Better to find new ways of achieving its goals.

item.title: Why do I have to tip 20 percent on an expensive meal? Your dining questions, answered.
item.description: Washington Post food critic Elazar Sontag entertained readers’ restaurant questions, rants and raves.

item.title: Trump moves to remake Dulles Airport, weighing redesign proposals
item.description: The administration, which is convening companies today, is considering plans that include building a new terminal and doing away with the sometimes notorious shuttles known as people movers.

item.title: How to support couple friends rocked by cheating. Carolyn Hax readers give advice.
item.description: How to support couple friends rocked by cheating. Hax readers give advice.

item.title: A bonkers 37.9 inches of snow hit this Northeast city. Here's how that happened.
item.description: It would be a shocking amount of snow falling just about anywhere in the Northeast, and the total observed in Providence, Rhode Island, is more common of locations farther inland, though even there it is quite a rarity.

item.title: A workout in jeans in today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Wednesday points.

item.title: Bar patrons at a State of the Union watch party drank free until Trump’s first insult. The beer flowed longer than expected.
item.description: The kegs were ready. Revelers crammed against the glossy white bar. Any jab targeting “an individual, a group of people or a country,” as the event page defined it, would halt the flow of gratis suds.

item.title: The moment Trump’s State of the Union address changed
item.description: A consciously made-for-TV moment marked an inflection point in the president’s speech — from a first hour of sunny optimism to a second hour of partisan barbs and graphic descriptions of violence.

item.title: State of the Union. Trump poll. East Wing. Nancy Guthrie. Snowball fight. AI cheating. All-or-nothing thinking. Catch up now.
item.description: Catch up in minutes with these 7 stories.

item.title: ‘A really important moment and question for the country’: 10 writers on Trump’s State of the Union address
item.description: Follow columnists as they discuss Trump’s State of the Union address

item.title: Trump is set to deliver his first State of the Union address since returning to office. Follow our coverage.
item.description: Polls show that Americans largely remain sour about the president’s performance more than a year since his inauguration, and Trump is expected to use his prime-time speaking slot to outline how he’s delivered on the agenda he was elected on.

item.title: Federal judge says government may not search devices seized from Washington Post reporter
item.description: The ruling suggested that Magistrate Judge William Porter did not trust the government to conduct a narrow search of the devices and feared that such an examination could risk exposing more than 1,000 of reporter Hannah Natanson’s secretive government sources to the Justice Department.

item.title: As measles cases climb, these 9 vaccine-preventable diseases are also threatening comebacks
item.description: When vaccination rates decrease, the most highly contagious diseases pop up first, making measles "the canary in the coal mine," an expert said.

item.title: Trump administration considers requiring banks to collect citizenship information
item.description: The White House is exploring measures that could require banks to collect additional citizenship information from customers, according to four people familiar with the matter. It raises concerns that the move could create new barriers to financial services.

item.title: Hegseth threatens to force Anthropic to share its AI technology in the name of national security
item.description: Anthropic is reportedly prepared to walk away from negotiations — and its $200 million contract with the Defense Department — if concerns over the use of its technology for autonomous weapons or mass surveillance are not addressed.

item.title: The best and worst things Trump has done, according to a survey of 2,300 Americans
item.description: Immigration was the most divisive issue, with supporters calling it President Donald Trump’s best action and critics his worst.

item.title: Tariffs were illegal. Now Trump wants to delay refunds.
item.description: The government said small businesses would be made whole. It’s time to pay up.

item.title: Behind closed doors, GOP lawmaker questioned ‘disturbing’ East Wing demolition
item.description: Rep. Michael Turner, co-chair of the congressional Historic Preservation Caucus, wrote to a top White House aide about his “substantial concerns” as party leaders publicly backed Trump’s decision.

item.title: D.C. attorney general says Congress missed deadline, can’t block tax policy
item.description: Congress voted to swat down D.C.’s attempt to opt out of Trump’s tax cuts, but D.C. Attorney General Brian Schwalb (D) says Congress acted too late.

item.title: Family of Nancy Guthrie offers $1 million reward as hope of recovery dims
item.description: In a tearful video, the missing 84-year-old’s daughter, “Today” anchor Savannah Guthrie, pleaded with the public for help.

item.title: Remembering McSteamy in today’s On the Record
item.description: Answer now to earn your Tuesday points.
